original_abstract: In this 2011 biennial review, the IUPAC Commission on Isotopic Abundances and Atomic Weights has updated the standard atomic weights for five chemical elements based on recent high-precision measurements and evaluations of isotopic variability in nature. Significant revisions include the transition of bromine and magnesium to interval notation—[79.901, 79.907] and [24.304, 24.307], respectively—to better represent the natural variations of their atomic weights in terrestrial materials. Additionally, the standard atomic weights for germanium, indium, and mercury have been refined to $72.630(8)$, $114.818(1)$, and $200.592(3)$. This report details the methodology for determining these intervals and provides updated tables for all elements, including conventional atomic-weight values for practical use in trade and commerce. The report also highlights the development of the "Periodic Table of the Isotopes" as a new educational tool and catalogs the relative atomic masses and half-lives of selected radionuclides.
humanized_abstract: The IUPAC Commission on Isotopic Abundances and Atomic Weights recently released their 2011 biennial review of the standard atomic weights of the elements. The commission has changed the standard atomic weights of five elements because of more recent analyses of higher accuracy measurements of isotopic abundances and of the ranges of isotopic compositions found in natural terrestrial samples of the elements. For bromine and magnesium the standard atomic weights have been changed to the interval notation and are given as [79.901, 79.907] and [24.304, 24.307] respectively. The standard atomic weights of germanium, indium, and mercury were changed to 72.630(8), 114.818(1), and 200.592(3), respectively. The report describes the changes and includes atomic weights in their conventional format together with the full ranges of all the atomic weight data needed for trade and commerce. An updated "Periodic Table of the Isotopes" is also introduced as an educational tool. Tables of relative atomic masses and half-lives are given for selected radionuclides.
